subject: Surgery
title: Thyroid surgery
preambleMarkdown:
# Thyroid surgery



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Thyroid surgery is a procedure or practical clinical workflow in surgery.
- This article focuses on indications, anatomy, perioperative risk, operative options, postoperative care, and complications.
- The practical clinical priority is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Thyroid surgery to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Thyroid surgery is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnostic, therapeutic, emergent, urgent, or elective use.
- Bedside, image-guided, endoscopic, operative, or hybrid approach.
- Low-risk versus high-risk patient based on bleeding, infection, airway, anatomy, anticoagulation, and comorbid disease.
- Initial procedure, repeat procedure, failed procedure, or complication-management setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Thyroid surgery depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In surgery, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Thyroid surgery.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Thyroid surgery disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Thyroid surgery should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Thyroid surgery first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Thyroid surgery, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The clinical presentation is the problem that makes the procedure necessary.
- Assess severity, stability, anatomy, bleeding risk, infection risk, allergies, medications, and prior procedures.
- The most important history is what changes indication, urgency, consent, sedation, or aftercare.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Thyroid surgery, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused surgery examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Thyroid surgery presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Thyroid surgery with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Thyroid surgery is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Thyroid surgery should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm indication, alternatives, risks, consent, equipment, positioning, monitoring, and post-procedure plan.
- Correct modifiable risks when time allows, especially anticoagulation, coagulopathy, infection, and unstable physiology.
- Stop or escalate if anatomy is unclear, complications develop, or the intended endpoint is not reached.

### Initial Management

- Confirm the indication, urgency, consent pathway, patient identity, site, equipment, and monitoring plan for Thyroid surgery.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Perform the procedure only when the indication, anatomy, equipment, operator skill, and rescue plan are acceptable.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Thyroid surgery, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Thyroid surgery may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Thyroid surgery patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Thyroid surgery, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Thyroid surgery in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Thyroid surgery, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Thyroid surgery does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Thyroid surgery may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Thyroid surgery, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Thyroid surgery as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the indication, contraindication, key anatomy, major complication, and aftercare.
- Document neurovascular status, bleeding risk, consent, and response when relevant.
- Never let the desire to complete a procedure override patient stability or unsafe anatomy.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Thyroid surgery draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

```text
THYROID
I. Anatomy and Physiology
A. Anatomy
. . . - . -
l . Arterial blood supply
a. The superior thyroid artery originates from the external carotid artery,
which is the first branch beyond bifurcation of the common carotid artery.

b. The inferior thyroid artery is a branch of the thyrocervical trunk of the
subclavian artery.

2. Venous blood supply
a. The superior and inferior thyroid veins are paired with the superior and
inferior thyroid arteries.

b . All venous drainage is to the internal jugular vein.

3. Nerve supply
a. Recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN)
( 1 ) A branch of the vagus nerve that descends along the internal carotid
artery, the RLN is located in close proximity to the inferior thyroid
artery branches.

(a) The right RLN loops around the subclavian artery.

(b) The left RLN loops around the aortic arch.

(2) The RLN innervates all intrinsic muscles of the larynx except the
cricothyroideus.

(3) The tubercle of Zuckerkandl (pyramidal extension of superior thyroid
tissue) and the notch of the cricothyroid membrane are landmarks for
RLN insertion.

b . External branch of superior laryngeal nerve (EBSLN)
( 1 ) The superior laryngeal nerve is a branch of the vagus nerve and divides
into external and internal branches.

(2) The EBSLN innervates the cricothyroideus muscle and inferior pharyn
geal constrictor.

(3) The internal branch provides sensation to the larynx above the vocal
cords . Injury results in the inability to phonate in high-pitched sound,
yelling, and "voice fatigue." This is described as the voice becoming
softer at the end of the day or with prolonged use.

4. Anomalies of thyroid development
a. Lingual thyroid: Thyroid tissue at the base of the tongue may require
resection.

b . Thyroglossal duct cyst: This is located in the midline between the base
of the tongue and the isthmus, and resection may require removal of the
central portion of the hyoid bone.

c. Pyramidal lobe: should be excised during operation to avoid excess tissue
as antigenic stimulus in Graves disease, hindrance to remnant ablation in
cancer, or persistent hormone production in hyperthyroidism
T h e R L N controls the v o c a l

16 9

B. Physiology
l . Makes thyroid hormones-triiodothyronine (T3) and thyroxine (T4) from
iodine and tyrosine. T3 and T4 are stored in the gland when bound to
thyroglobulin.

a. The secretion of T3 and T4 is under the control of thyrotropin-releasing
hormone (TRH) secreted by the hypothalamus, which regulates thyroid
stimulating hormone (TSH) from the anterior pituitary.

b. Increased plasma levels of T3 and T4 result in negative feedback on TRH
and TSH.

## 2 . Calcitonin is secreted by parafollicular or C-cells, which are involved in

medullary thyroid cancer.

II. Hyperthyroidism [Table 1 0-l l
A. Causes
l . Graves disease (diffuse toxic goiter)
T4 is s e c reted exclusively
by the thyroid g l a n d . T3 is
mainly p r o d u c e d by p e r i p h
e r a l c o nversion of T 4 by d e
i o d i n a s e i n the liver, m u s c l e ,
a n d k i d n ey.

a. Autoimmune disorder with development of antibodies that stimulate
thyroid hormone production or secretion
b. Hyperthyroid symptoms include nervousness, irritability, heat intolerance,
involuntary weight loss, palpitations, sweating, and tachycardia secondary to
enhanced effect of catecholamines. It is also associated with exophthalmos,
edema of the eyelids, pretibial edema, hypertension, arrhythmias,
osteoporosis, and amenorrhea.

c. The TSH level is suppressed, and T3 and T4 levels are elevated.

d. Radioactive iodine (RAI) scan and tracer uptake are diagnostic.

2. Hashimoto thyroiditis (hashitoxicosis)
a. Five percent of patients with Hashimoto thyroiditis are hyperthyroid.

b . This is generally a transient process, and eventually, hypothyroidism
ensues.

## 3 . Toxic multinodular goiter

a. This is adenomatous hyperplasia of the thyroid gland.

b. The most common type of multinodular goiter is usually euthyroid.

4. Toxic adenoma: Treatment is RAI ablation or lobectomy for control of hyper
thyroidism .

B. Medical management
l . Antithyroid drugs: propylthiouracil (PTU) , methimazole, and Tapazole
a. Block organification of iodine and formation of thyroid hormone.

b . Prevent peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 by deiodinase (PTU) .

2. Complications include aplastic anemia and liver function abnormalities.

3 . Failure also occurs with noncompliance.

4. Beta-blockers control symptoms mediated by catecholamines.

C. RAI
l . I- 1 3 1 destroys the thyrocytes with minimal effect to surrounding structures. Its
therapeutic effect is not immediate. Sometimes, it requires repeat treatment.

2. Relative contraindications include large gland, Graves ophthalmopathy, con
cerns for radiation exposure in the pregnant patient or the patient considering
pregnancy in the near future, and the risk of hyperparathyroidism.

D. Surgical management: near-total thyroidectomy
l . Preoperative preparation for surgery
2. This most often includes beta-blockers to prevent thyroid storm and control
symptoms. It may be necessary to taper these drugs postoperatively.

3. Lugol solution (iodine solution) orally to decrease vascularity of the thyroid
gland and decrease hyperthyroidism
Ill. Goiter
A. Iodine deficiency is a major cause of goiter.

B. ClassiHcation is by function or anatomy (toxic, nontoxic, diffuse, focal, and smooth) .

l . Primary
a. Intrathoracic location (substernal)
b. Intrathoracic blood supply
c. Venous drainage into the chest
d. No connection to the cervical thyroid gland

## 2. Secondary

a. Direct extension of normally located tissue in the neck
b. Normal blood supply

## 3 . Intrathoracic or substernal goiters

a. From 1 0% to 1 5 % of these goiters are primary.

b. If located in a posterior position, they are associated with symptoms of
compression of esophagus and displacement of trachea. This location is
most likely at the thoracic inlet at the level of the sternal notch secondary
to bony conHnement.

c. Diagnosis of airway compromise involves a flow-loop spirogram on
pulmonary function test.

C. Treatment
l . Near-total thyroidectomy is the mainstay when symptoms arise.

2. Most can be done through a cervical incision, rarely require median sternotomy.

D. Symptomatic goiters
l . Most goiters are multinodular and benign, and patients are euthyroid. The
presence of cancer within a goiter is less than 1 % , and within a multinodular
goiter 2% to 4% .

2. Symptoms of aerodigestive obstruction should be sought.

3. Dysphagia, odynophagia, dyspnea, orthopnea, and changes in these symptoms
related to position of head, neck, and arms should be assessed.

4. Persistent and consistent hoarseness warrants further evaluation to exclude
nerve involvement by tumor.

IV. Thyroid Nodule
A. This discrete lesion within the thyroid gland is palpably anor ultrasonographi
cally distinct from the surrounding parenchyma. These nodules are palpable in
5% of the population.

B. In the United States, approximately 50% of individuals older than 50 years have
thyroid nodules by ultrasonic evaluation (more in women than in men) .

C. Less than 5% of nodules prove to be malignant. The risk of malignancy is related
to history of radiation, age (extremes of age lead to greater malignancy) , number
of nodules (solitary more worrisome) , and sex (men are more likely to have
malignant nodules) .

D . Diagnosis
l . Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) biopsy: The most accurate, cost-effective method
for evaluating thyroid nodules , this is the procedure of choice .

a. FNA is safe , minimally invasive, and accurate. The false-negative rate for a
benign Hnding is less than 5%.

b . FNA should be performed in any nodule greater than l.O em.

H e rthle cell c h a n g e in
fo l l i c u l a r nodules is more
likely to be m a l i g n a nt and
s h o u l d b e s u r g i c a l l y resecte d .

F N A s h a v e a h i g h e r
fa l s e  n e g ative rate i n
patie nts w i t h p r i o r h e a d o r
n e c k radiati o n ; thus, these
patie nts should h a ve a
s u r g i c a l resection if they
deve l o p a thyroid n o d u l e .

c. Diagnostic categories
( 1 ) Nondiagnostic or inadequate: Repeat FNA. If Hrst attempt not done
under ultrasound guidance, do so now (more than two repetitions is of
limited benefit) .

(2) Indeterminate: Consider repeat FNA or follow with serial ultrasound.

If there is an increase in size, consider repeat FNA, or total thyroidec
tomy or lobectomy.

(3) Malignant: Perform surgical excision.

(4) Benign: No further diagnostic studies or treatment is necessary.

## 2. Ultrasound

a. Accurate at determining size, number, and character (solid vs. cystic) of
nodules
b. Used to diagnose multinodular goiter
c. Used to assess malignant features : calcifications, irregular borders , increased
blood flow
3. Radionuclide scanning
a. Done with radioiodine or technetium-99m pertechnetate
b. Rarely useful for evaluation of nodular disease (ultrasound and TSH allow
accurate prediction of what scan will show)
4. Measurement of TSH and a history of symptoms (mobility, firmness, solitary
nodule, adenopathy, voice changes) are used to exclude toxic adenoma.

5. Thyroid function tests are of little value because most nodules are nonfunctional.

E. Treatment: Indications for surgical removal without use of ultrasound or FNA
include:
l. Tracheoesophageal compressive symptoms of a nodule
2. History of significant radiation exposure or radiation therapy to head and neck
or mediastinum (nodule in this setting has a 30% risk of malignancy)
3. Large nodule (solid or cystic) greater than 4 em with or without symptoms,
which carries high risk of malignancy (FNA may have sampling error due to size)
V. Thyroid Cancer
A. The incidence is 1/10 ,000 per year, with deaths occurring in 1/200,000 individuals
per year (Table 1 0-2) .

B. Well-differentiated cancers (approximately 95% of all; papillary 80% and
follicular 1 5%)
l. Ten percent are associated with a history of radiation exposure (typically
papillary variant) .

2. These cancers most commonly present as asymptomatic thyroid nodules.

## 3 . Treatment involves surgery. Lobectomy versus total thyroidectomy:

a. Advantages of lobectomy
( l ) Eliminates possibility of permanent hypoparathyroidism
(2) Decreases the risk of nerve injury
(3) Does not require thyroid hormone replacement
b. Disadvantages of lobectomy
E N D O C R I N E S U R G E R Y e 1 7 3
( l ) Most physicians would recommend thyroid hormone suppression
for prevention of recurrence of benign or malignant nodule in the
remaining lobe.

(2) Thyroglobulin cannot be used as a tumor marker as evidence of
recurrence of cancer.

(3) Whole-body RAI scanning cannot be performed to look for recurrent
or metastatic disease .

4. Follow-up involves serial neck examinations and ultrasound, thyroglobulin,
and RAI scans if total thyroidectomy was performed in patients at moderate to
high risk of recurrence.

5. Prognosis makes use of prognostic scoring indices to stratify patients into
three risk groups-low- , intermediate-, and high-risk. AMES/AGES and
MACIS age is 40 years for men and 50 years for women ( 45 years for both in
tumor-node-metastases system) .

a. AMES: age, metastases, extent, and size
b. AGES: age, grade, extent, and size (limitation is subj ective interpretation of
pathologic grade)
c. MACIS: metastases , age , completeness of resection, invasion, and size
C. Medullary thyroid carcinoma (approximately 4%)
l . Total thyroidectomy with ipsilateral functional neck dissection is indicated.

2. No sex or age predilection: This form of thyroid carcinoma is sporadic in 60%
to 70% of cases and familial in the rest.

a. Sporadic cases typically have a single focus with spread to the lymphatics.

b. Familial cases are typically multifocal and more aggressive.

( l ) These cases carry a worse prognosis . However, even in the face of
metastatic disease (miliary liver metastases) , patients can live for more
than 10 years.

(2) They can also be associated with multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN)
type II syndrome.

(3) Spread occurs through the lymphatics and bloodstream.

3. The tumors secrete calcitonin, so calcitonin is followed as a tumor marker.

4. Serial ultrasounds are followed to look for recurrence. If there is no evidence
of neck disease and consistently rising calcitonin levels , multiple small liver
metastases should be considered.

5. Medullary thyroid carcinoma has a parafollicular C-cell origin, and therefore,
RAI and thyroglobulin are not effective in following or treating disease .

6. Medullary thyroid cancer has a poorer prognosis than well-differentiated
thyroid cancer.

D. Lymphoma, anaplastic, and metastatic thyroid carcinoma are rare (l %) .

l . Lymphoma: may present like thyroiditis with rapidly enlarging tender gland
a. A rare condition, it affects mainly older women (aged 50 to 70 years) .

b. Open biopsy may be required to distinguish from thyroiditis. FNA shows
lymphocytes and thyroid follicles.

c. If focal, it can be treated with radiation therapy.

d. If diffuse, it can be treated with chemotherapy.

e. The prognosis is variable and depends on the type and extent of disease.

## 2 . Anaplastic thyroid cancer: one of the most aggressive cancers ; usually presents

with advanced disease
a. This form of cancer affects the older population (aged 50 to 70 years) , and
it has no sex predilection.

b . It is rapidly growing, with early invasion of adjacent structures .

c. Metastasis occurs early via lymphatics and bloodstream.

d. It is usually incurable with a poor prognosis .

( l ) Surgical resection is usually palliative and for control of respiratory
```
